engines_involved.role: primary — auto-creates a vuln workflow remediation ticket for every finding
engines_involved.instantiation: VulnWorkflowEngine.for_org(org_id) — uses classmethod factory, not bare constructor

trigger_sources: SecurityFindingsEngine when a new finding is recorded

current_state.summary: Single-engine flow. VulnWorkflowEngine exists with the correct for_org() classmethod factory (vuln_workflow_engine.py:111) and create_ticket() method (vuln_workflow_engine.py:234). The instantiation pattern matches what the subscriber uses. Severity is validated against {critical, high, medium, low} with fallback 'medium'. source_engine is validated against an 8-value allowlist.
current_state.isolation: _safe_call wraps the ticket creation so failures are logged and swallowed.
current_state.cve_id_handling: cve_id is extracted from event_data and passed through to ticket data — VulnWorkflowEngine stores it as a field on the ticket.

what_works: VulnWorkflowEngine.for_org(org_id) factory correctly scopes the engine to the org.
create_ticket() is called with title, severity, source_engine, and cve_id.
Severity normalisation prevents invalid values reaching the DB.
source_engine allowlist prevents unexpected source strings from being stored.
Legacy bus is registered for EventType.FINDING_CREATED.
Both TrustGraph and legacy bus registrations are in place.
what_doesnt: No deduplication — the same finding_id can generate multiple tickets if the event fires more than once (e.g. on rescan without status change).
No reverse link — the ticket created does not store a reference back to the originating finding's ID in the source engine.
entity_id is used only in the ticket title string, not stored as a structured foreign key field.
No ALERT_CREATED event is emitted after ticket creation for critical/high findings, missing an opportunity to trigger the ALERT_CREATED fan-out.
source_engine fallback to 'manual' is silent — no warning is logged when an unknown source is coerced.
The flow does not trigger VulnPrioritizationEngine or VulnerabilityRemediationEngine, so SLA timers do not start automatically.
No test in the Beast Mode suite exercises this subscriber.
acceptance_criteria: AC1: Given a FINDING_CREATED event with severity='critical' and source_engine='scanner', VulnWorkflowEngine.create_ticket() is called exactly once with matching org_id, severity, and source_engine.
AC2: Given a FINDING_CREATED event with source_engine='unknown_tool', the ticket is created with source_engine='manual' and a WARNING log entry is emitted.

AC3: Given a FINDING_CREATED event carrying a valid cve_id (e.g. 'CVE-2024-1234'), the created ticket must store that cve_id as a structured field retrievable via GET /api/v1/vuln-workflow/tickets/{id}.
AC4: Duplicate suppression — if the same (org_id, entity_id, source_engine) fires twice within 60 seconds, only one ticket is created.
AC5: For critical/high severity findings, an ALERT_CREATED event is emitted after ticket creation so the ALERT_CREATED fan-out can escalate to incident if needed.
AC6: The ticket must store a structured finding_source_id field linking back to the originating entity_id.
AC7: A pytest test covers both valid and invalid source_engine values, and the cve_id passthrough.
AC8: Failure of VulnWorkflowEngine must be caught, logged at WARNING level, and must not propagate an exception to the caller.
